speaker: interviewer
text: It's March 3rd, 1996. So you were describing that you-- in April of 1945, you were transported back to Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Describe Theresienstadt the second time around.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, let me tell you, on the train, when we got to Bauschovitz, which is a big, big train station, we were standing there and standing there and waiting. And we knew we were going to Theresienstadt. We couldn't understand why. Because-- excuse me-- we knew that people never get to the same camp twice. But then it turned out that the Pol-- Polish girls and the Hungarians were 300, and we were only 200. So they were more than us, so then we came back. And we're standing there and standing there until, I believe, they got the permission to unload us. And the SS women were with us. We-- we were not fed during the time or whatever. We were fed in a way that people throw things at us-- food. And maybe we got each a little piece of bread, but, I mean, this is not very much to sustain you for about 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a 10-day trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they tried to attach some-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] lots of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trains where wounded people, wounded soldiers were. And they tried to attach it to our train, because they realized that the Americans would not bomb us because they had realized that the planes going back and forth-- the Americans-- had seen that we were who we were. It was an open cattle car. And-- so we escaped narrowly quite some scrapes. And standing there and standing there. Come the Americans and bomb this train yard. And it was terribly scary, because we didn't know how good they were at bombing. They were coming. They dived in, ba, ba, ba, ba, ba, bombed one train, made a loop, and came back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as nearer as they came to our train, as more afraid we were. Do they really see us? You know. Do they really know how we are-- who we are? And they really looped it and took the next one to the right again. It was terribly loud. It was terrible, terrible. But we were so afraid that, in the very last minute, we're getting bombed. So having all that behind us, we had to walk back to Theresienstadt, which is quite a walk. And this is when we were so very thirsty, and it had been raining. And no-- no way we had-- could get anything to drink. And I remember a big puddle, and we drank out of the puddle. And that was-- we didn't think it was demeaning. We were just glad that there was water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we walked and walked and walked and walked. And it was so long to Theresienstadt. And they housed us in the catacombs, because they were very afraid that we all have typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But before we got into Theresienstadt, before we got into the catacombs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the commandant, Mr. Rahm, came, and looked in-- looked at us. He had seen us. He knew us from a year before, or half a year before. And he knew some people by name. And he could not understand what had happened to us. And all we would tell him was, take these women and put them on the [GERMAN]. They were so mean to us. And Mr. Rahm, knowing then well that his end was near, he rather did what these people were telling him. And all the SS women were taken to-- to the [GERMAN] to the prison. And then, finally, we could go to the catacombs. And we got a little food here and there. But we could hardly walk anymore. I mean, our strength was gone. And from there-- there we rested, I think, a day or so. And then we were brought into wooden barracks which were in front of Theresienstadt, because they were afraid we all had typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- then we slowly recuperated, very slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food and drink at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we had to be very careful, because when you eat too much, you get sick. And you could only eat very, very small portions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somebody who I happen to know saw me and said, you know, I bring your food, but you can't eat much. And so brought me food a few times a day, just little teeny portions.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned drinking out of a puddle. At any time while you were in the various camps, were you were able to see yourself, to know what you looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In Theresienstadt I did, but that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the second time you were there, or the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time. Well, in Auschwitz, of course, we were all shaved. But we-- you know, in the window you could see yourself, but that's all. All the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you look like to yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yuck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No hair, strange hats. You couldn't-- you could not distinguish people. They all looked  the same. Now we all were just going like this to see what our hair growing in again. No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second time you were at Theresienstadt, were you given any work to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your daily routine like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to get up, and move around very slowly. And the commandant came once a day and looked. How-- are you feeling better? Et cetera, et cetera. By the same token, also, the Swiss Red Cross was there. And Mr. [INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name of the man who was there. And he took letters. He said, anybody who has any letter to anybody or wants to write letters to anybody, anywhere in the world, he would-- he would mail it. And he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you send a letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I sent two letters, and they got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who?

speaker: interviewee
text: One to a family Kaufman in Basel, and one to my aunt in London. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The one to my aunt in London [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to my uncle, was merely to tell them whatever you tried, I am alive. And I pay you back for what you did to me. That was-- that was the purpose of my letter to my aunt. And the other-- people really tried to-- to help us later, Kaufmans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything from your second stay at Theresienstadt that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only the [INAUDIBLE]. The controversy of the Germans being very afraid when the war would be ending, and the Swiss Red Cross being there and trying to give us better food. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think we had missed the three [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bosses from the Danish-- from the Danish King. We just missed them, so the Danish people were gone already. And it was just, again, very weird and very different. But we had to stay in our barracks. We could go around after about two month-- two weeks-- and-- when we were stronger. And we didn't need to work, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at all notified, or did you find out when Hitler died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't told about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only news we had was when we went [INAUDIBLE], we would tell these stupid women, these SS women, that we didn't like what they had on their radio, and they should move to another station. And they told us they don't understand it. And we said, but we like it. And they were so ridiculous. They didn't realize that it was the news which we wanted to hear in Czech. [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew that. But when he died and all this, we had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were coming. The Russians were coming. We heard that they were coming, and we all tried to get out of Theresienstadt simply to see them come in. And the people on the way-- you know, in these little houses [INAUDIBLE] Bauschovitz. And they were very nice. They were so glad to see us and have something to drink and have some milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was really a feeling that's unbelievable. Here came the Russians. They looked-- some of them were sitting on their bicycles like they would be the Huguenots sitting there on their little horses. You know, you were thinking, do they have [INAUDIBLE] or not? You know, they're very odd people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we soon realized that we didn't understand each other at all. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we wanted to have nothing to do with them. They tried to rape, and this and that and the other. But you had to fight them off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever try to attack you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Successfully, I-- [LAUGHS] We-- we had-- you never had so many uses for hangers in your life. And-- but it worked. And-- I must say, one of them I shot, because he also got a little too close to me. And somebody gave me-- one of the Chetniks gave me a small revolver for safe keeping. It was wrong. When this man approached me-- I didn't care if it was a Russian or what it was. But I shot him. I didn't think anything of it. Except I went to the Russian commandant, and I said, there is a dead Russian in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what happened? He must have gotten shot. That worked out [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, it's my life or you life. Sorry, it's yours, you know, rather than mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't stay there too long, because beginning of July, the beginning of-- no. I was in Hamburg. The beginning of June, three boys-- three guys and I went on Russian military trains to Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from Berlin, I came to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Hamburg look like to you when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Incredible. You know, it was bombed, this wasn't here. I couldn't even find my way into Hamburg when they wanted to drive me there. It took a long while to find the right streets, or the right passage to go get in there. It was-- just looked differently. It was very bombed in there, you know, the way you get into Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finally find your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finally, and how did you finally find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to my uncle. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he told me where my mother was, and she was-- they had-- they had picked her up in Bergen-Belsen. And since he didn't have any room for her, they rented a room for my mother someplace near where our apartment was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I got there at 8:00 in the morning and-- I didn't want to be too early. It was Sunday. I rang the bell and asked for my mother, and she was there. And she looked at me and said, who are you? Did you recognize her right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she look to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Similar to my mother. But when I tried to explain to her what I was-- who I was, she just denied it. She said, no. She heard I was a Russian territory. I wouldn't come back so quickly. So that was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What finally convinced her that you were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slapped her crazy. I slapped her to be-- to wake up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And many people were in the room, all the people who had brought me-- these com-- these people in uniform, et cetera. And I knew I had to do something which I really don't like to do. I had to slap her. Slap her face until she wakes up. My [INAUDIBLE] daughter, what are you doing here? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the two of you do to rebuild your lives? How did you start over?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no room for me except in the Jewish hospital, where I got a bed. And then when we-- we decided we want to go back to our apartment. And we took a lawyer, and we went back to our apartment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told that my little school friend was there almost every day after the war, and asking the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] superintendent, have I come back already? And she said, no. We don't know anything. That is, she wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were three women living in this apartment-- four, rather. It was all bombed out on top. So since it was a duplex, the top was all gone. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these-- one woman was the one who rented it originally with her husband, and then she had to take in boarders, two. And one of them had a friend there. And the one with a friend there was very nice, and said, OK, we'll leave right away. It's your house, et cetera, et cetera. And the-- the other one-- the one who we rented it-- who had rented it-- she said she would get out of it as soon as she can. And the other one said she's not going to leave until she finds something. And she was very mad. And I just told her, pack your stuff and leave right now. Call the police, but get the hell out of here. And so there were only three women there. And this one woman said, well, they moved someplace else. And this woman who rented it originally, she stayed in the apartment when we moved back in. But she had the room where it was always raining, because the roof was broken. So we ended up having her stay in our rooms, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what job did you finally find?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it took a long time until I was able to do anything. Then I worked for the English occupational forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cultural relations. And it was for a man called [INAUDIBLE], and who was quite a well-known author, was at this time. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the money was so worthless. For the money which I made in a month, I could buy for my mother a pound of butter, because she was very frail and very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I got here, some-- sometime in the '50s. In the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had the opportunity, as-- you know, as everybody was given, that they could leave. And my mother had remarried again. And Mr. [INAUDIBLE] had a friend who lived in New York, [INAUDIBLE]. And they persuaded me that I should come. And this is what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you find you were treated by the Germans after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them looked at us like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] green men from the moon. Oh god, they're back. Some of them in utter disgust that we didn't get killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And some of them were genuinely very happy to see us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you feel uncomfortable, some of these reactions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Of course. Would you like it when somebody tells you oh, you know, the weeds are back again? You know, [LAUGHS] what can you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with it? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Ignore it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the others, you don't need to see. You don't even need to have anything to do with them. And those who were genuinely happy, they're my friends. One of them, unfortunately, died about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three or four months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, what-- what did you do for a living here?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I worked at the household from these friends of Mr. [INAUDIBLE], and they paid me $10 a month-- a week. And I worked from 8:00 in the morning until 10:00 at night. And they figured it was a very good salary they gave me. And they lived in Great Neck, and they also had a farm in Afton, New York, and I had to work there, and clean their house, et cetera, et cetera, be their maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that was over-- and I really didn't like that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did I don't how many jobs. I worked as a sales girl. I worked as a laboratory person-- a man who was a nose. Do you know what a nose is? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: A man who makes perfumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there. And I worked for Welcome Wagon. And I can't tell you how many jobs I had. I don't think I have enough fingers and toes. Because people would hire me, but I didn't know anything. I didn't speak English too well, and I couldn't do this and I couldn't do that. So it was rather hard going for the first few years, or many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what job did you finally end up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the assistant of a public relations-- in a public relations agency. I was the assistant of the president and an account executive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned to me that-- in between tapes-- that you believe in God. How did living in the camps affect that belief?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I had two guardian angels [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because I made it through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Six million didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you were able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two guardian angels. That's all  I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those two being-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you have an idea who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You don't know who your guardian angels are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why two, I guess--

speaker: interviewee
text: One is too busy-- would have been too busy. He's too busy. [LAUGHTER] I still have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences in the Holocaust affect you afterwards, and even today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Subconsciously, I think, a lot. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I enjoy things much more than other people do. I don't take anything for granted. I don't even take tomorrow for granted. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I'm unable to make plans further than for three weeks from now. Sub-- subconsciously, I don't know whether I'm still alive. I don't know whether that makes any sense to you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, people plan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do that, because always I'm thinking, I'll never live to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about it often, the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A thought comes-- a train of thought comes from my mind once a day, but it just goes [INAUDIBLE]. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. Sometimes, in most inappropriate situations. But it's there. I mean, there are nights when I dream about it, but it's much, much less than in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you want to give to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regarding the Holocaust or what, or the life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never give up. Don't ever, ever, ever give up. That's most important. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And against all odds, do not give up. And the Holocaust is such-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] personally, it was a hard experience for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm very happy for the people who did not die, and very sad for those who couldn't stand it, or who died. Some people died out of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sadness about what was happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there is no other message for me to give [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] except you should never, ever, ever, ever give up.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war was over, were you finally able to have a birthday under the palm trees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, [LAUGHS] of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, where? Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Barbados, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the '70s. It was great. It was very hot. [LAUGHTER] It was great. Most enjoyable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dream come true. And I jumped around and danced around. I was so happy. People were-- what's the matter? Just because it's your birthday, you know? You can't tell everybody why you're so happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would want to add in closing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is my grandfather, Mr. Felix Schoenfeld. The picture was taken in Hamburg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my grandmother, Mrs. Annie Schoenfeld, born Feig, and the picture was taken in Hamburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a picture of my mother, Gerda Rodewilg, born Schoenfeld, and it was taken about 20 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's another picture of my mother, Gerda Rodewilg. It was taken 1990, so it's six years old. And I don't have a current picture of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Hamburg also?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is she still alive now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
